hook: The bare-bones Forcella temple of pizza that serves only marinara and margherita — and made Julia Roberts swoon in Eat Pray Love.
facts: The Condurro family pizza tradition dates to 1870; the pizzeria moved to its current Via Cesare Sersale 1 location in 1930.
It is famous for serving essentially only two pizzas: marinara and margherita.
It featured in the 2010 film Eat Pray Love, where Julia Roberts' character eats here, cementing its international fame.
It is widely cited as one of the oldest and most revered pizzerias in Naples, a reference point for Neapolitans and tourists for over 150 years.
legend: Locals say the spartan, no-frills room and refusal to expand the menu is itself the secret of its cult status.

sources.note: History: family tradition from 1870, Forcella 'temple of pizza'
Coordinates 40.849982, 14.2632526 and Via Cesare Sersale 1 address

w: A bare room in Forcella that has served essentially two pizzas — marinara and margherita — since the Condurro family settled here in 1930, their craft going back to 1870. No real menu, no frills, and that is the cult: Neapolitans queue, and Julia Roberts swooned over a margherita here in Eat Pray Love. The temple of the two pizzas.

hook: Naples' grand Belle Époque literary café on Piazza Trieste e Trento, where D'Annunzio, Wilde and Hemingway took their coffee.
facts: Founded in 1860, it was renamed 'Gran Caffè Gambrinus' after a major refurbishment, officially reinaugurated on 3 November 1890.
It became the heart of Neapolitan high society and literary life, frequented by Gabriele D'Annunzio, Benedetto Croce, Oscar Wilde, Matilde Serao and Ernest Hemingway.
In 1938 the fascist prefect Marziale ordered its closure as a suspected anti-fascist meeting place; it reopened after the war.
It sits on Via Chiaia 1, adjacent to Piazza Trieste e Trento and the Teatro San Carlo / Royal Palace.
legend: It is traditionally tied to the Neapolitan 'caffè sospeso' (suspended coffee) custom — paying for a stranger's coffee in advance.

sources.note: History: 1860 founding, 1890 renaming/inauguration, literary patrons, 1938 fascist closure

w: Naples' grand Belle Époque café on Piazza Trieste e Trento, pouring since 1860 and gilded in 1890 — coffee for D'Annunzio, Croce, Wilde, Serao and Hemingway. The fascist prefect shut it in 1938 as a nest of anti-fascists; it came back after the war. Order a caffè sospeso and pay for a stranger's too. History by the cup.

hook: The Via Toledo pastry shop where the sfogliatella riccia was born in 1818 — Naples' original shell-shaped pastry.
facts: Pintauro has operated on Via Toledo since 1785, originally as an inn before turning to pastry; it preserves marble decorations dated 1785.
In 1818 innkeeper Pasquale Pintauro created the sfogliatella, reworking a recipe from the Santa Rosa convent on the Amalfi Coast.
He later devised the shortcrust 'frolla' version to avoid the crumbling of the original flaky 'riccia'.
Its fame gave Naples the proverb 'Azz, tene folla Pintauro' (Wow, there's a crowd at Pintauro), used for anything that draws a throng.
It is located at Via Toledo 275 and reopened in March 2026 after a closure.
legend: The recipe is said to descend from a nun (in some accounts Pintauro's aunt, abbess of the Santa Rosa convent) who passed it to him.

sources.note: ANSA: 1818 birth of sfogliatella at Pintauro on Via Toledo

w: The sfogliatella was born behind this Via Toledo counter in 1818, when innkeeper Pasquale Pintauro reworked a recipe smuggled from an Amalfi-coast convent — the crisp, shell-layered riccia, and later the softer frolla. The shop has stood here since 1785, so beloved it gave Naples a proverb for any crowd: there's a queue at Pintauro's.

hook: A tiny, beloved Chiaia wine bar — a Neapolitan institution behind walls of wooden wine shelves.
facts: It began in the 1960s as a 'vini e oli', a rustic canteen serving simple dishes with local wine, and became a proper enoteca from the late 1980s.
It is described as an institution among residents of the upmarket Chiaia district.
It offers an extensive Italian wine list by the glass or bottle alongside two or three dishes each night.
It sits on Vico Belledonne a Chiaia 18, in the heart of Chiaia's nightlife alleys.

sources.note: History from 1960s 'vini e oli' to enoteca; institution of Chiaia
Coordinates 40.835127, 14.239428 for Vico Belledonne a Chiaia 18

w: A tiny wine bar down a Chiaia alley that started in the 1960s as a 'vini e oli,' a rough canteen of wine and a dish or two, and grew into a neighbourhood institution. Walls of wooden shelves, a long list by the glass, a couple of plates a night. The kind of local Chiaia keeps to itself. Squeeze in among the regulars.

hook: Naples' veteran record shop, trading vinyl in the centro storico since 1957.
facts: Erecord has been an institution in Neapolitan music since 1957, originally founded as Discoteca Meridionale.
Since 2012 the family kept only this historic shop open after closing its other outlets.
It is located in Vico Ferrovia, near the central station area, and stocks vinyl, CDs, cassettes and DVDs.
It now also runs erecord.it, an online catalogue of original records across formats.

sources.note: Founded 1957 (as Discoteca Meridionale); historic Vico Ferrovia shop kept open since 2012

w: Naples has bought its records here since 1957, when it opened as the Discoteca Meridionale near the central station. The family closed its other shops and kept this one — vinyl, CDs and tapes, deep stock down a centro storico lane. A survivor of the city's music trade, still digging. Records since the year of the 45.

hook: A vast tuff-cave ossuary of ~40,000 anonymous dead, where Neapolitans 'adopted' skulls and prayed to the abandoned souls.
facts: The ossuary holds roughly 40,000 remains in chambers carved from tuff quarries on the western edge of the Sanità district.
It received victims of the 1656 plague and the 1836 cholera epidemic, plus bones moved from church burial vaults.
In 1872 canon Gaetano Barbati had the bones arranged by type, and the site opened to the public.
It became the centre of the 'anime pezzentelle' cult, where working-class women adopted a skull (the 'capuzzella') and prayed for the soul in exchange for graces.
The Church suppressed the cult and the site was closed in 1969; it sits on Via delle Fontanelle 80.
legend: The most famous adopted skull is 'Lucia', venerated as the soul of a young bride; petitioners still leave offerings at the capuzzelle.

sources.note: Coordinates 40.858842, 14.238764; address Via delle Fontanelle 80; 1656 plague, 1872 reorganisation, 1969 closure
Cult of anime pezzentelle, ~40,000 dead, capuzzelle adoption practice

w: Forty thousand anonymous dead in a tuff cave under the Sanità — plague victims of 1656, cholera dead of 1836, bones cleared from church vaults. From it grew the cult of the anime pezzentelle: women adopting a skull, a 'capuzzella,' to pray for its abandoned soul in return for grace. The Church suppressed it and shut the caves in 1969.

hook: The Baroque funerary chapel of the alchemist-prince Raimondo di Sangro, home to Sanmartino's astonishing Veiled Christ.
facts: The chapel was begun in the late 1500s as the di Sangro family burial temple; Raimondo di Sangro, 7th Prince of Sansevero, remade it from c.1750, with works completed in 1766.
Its centrepiece is the Cristo Velato (Veiled Christ), carved in marble by Giuseppe Sanmartino in 1753, famed for its translucent 'veil'.
Raimondo di Sangro was an inventor, anatomist and Grand Master of Neapolitan Freemasonry.
Its crypt holds the 'Macchine Anatomiche': two skeletons with an artificially preserved circulatory system whose technique remains debated.
It is located at Via Francesco De Sanctis 19/21, off Spaccanapoli.
legend: Legend claims the marble veil was a real cloth 'marmorised' by the prince's alchemy, and that he had the anatomical machines' arteries injected while their subjects were alive.

sources.note: Coordinates 40.84919, 14.25488; Via De Sanctis 19/21; Veiled Christ, anatomical machines, Raimondo di Sangro
History of the prince and chapel, between history and legend

w: The funerary chapel the alchemist-prince Raimondo di Sangro rebuilt around 1750 — and the marble miracle at its heart, Sanmartino's 1753 Veiled Christ, its shroud so sheer the body shows through. In the crypt, two skeletons strung with an eerily preserved circulatory system. Legend says the prince marbleised a real veil over his Christ.

hook: Forcella's 'theatre of the Neapolitan song' — the working-class stage where the sceneggiata napoletana came of age.
facts: Inaugurated on 8 November 1911 at the edge of Forcella, it was conceived as a popular venue, a deliberate contrast to elite theatres like the San Carlo.
It centred on the tradition of Neapolitan song and became a principal home of the sceneggiata napoletana (music, drama and dialect poetry).
It hosted the De Filippo, Viviani and Taranto families, as well as Totò and singer Elvira Donnarumma.
It later became the Splendore cinema (1947) before being restored and reopened as a theatre in 2002, dedicated to playwright Raffaele Viviani.
It is a 530-seat Italian-style theatre at Piazza Vincenzo Calenda 9, today branded the 'Teatro della Canzone Napoletana'.

sources.note: Coordinates 40.85042, 14.263346; Piazza Calenda 9; 1911 inauguration, sceneggiata, 2002 revival
Official history: theatre of the Neapolitan song, Forcella

w: Forcella's theatre of the Neapolitan song, opened in 1911 as a deliberately working-class answer to the gilded San Carlo. Here the sceneggiata napoletana came of age — music, melodrama and dialect — with Totò, the De Filippos and the Viviani family on its boards. A spell as a cinema, then back to a theatre in 2002. The people's stage.
